era_context: The early 14th century saw the fragmentation of Mongol power into regional khanates, the consolidation of Ottoman expansion in Anatolia, the height of the Mali Empire in West Africa, and the late medieval period in Europe marked by dynastic conflicts, papal authority disputes, and the early stirrings of the Renaissance in Italy. The Yuan Dynasty under Temür Khan ruled China; the Delhi Sultanate dominated South Asia; and the Byzantine Empire clung to Constantinople and its hinterland.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa beyond Mali and the East African coast
Southeast Asian kingdoms (Khmer, Sukhothai, early Java)

events.title: Battle of Bileća: Ottoman victory over Serbian forces
events.description: Ottoman forces under Osman I defeated Serbian and Bosnian troops at Bileća, consolidating Ottoman control over the western Balkans. This victory marked a significant step in Ottoman expansion into former Byzantine and Serbian territories. The battle demonstrated the military effectiveness of Ottoman cavalry and contributed to the gradual Ottoman conquest of the Balkans over the following century.
events.certainty_note: The battle is attested in Ottoman and Serbian sources, but dating to 1301 specifically is conventional; some sources place it slightly earlier or later. Contemporary documentation is absent.

events.title: Papal Bull Unam Sanctam issued by Boniface VIII
events.description: Pope Boniface VIII issued the bull Unam Sanctam, asserting papal supremacy over secular rulers and claiming the Church's authority over temporal as well as spiritual matters. This was the culmination of his conflict with King Philip IV of France over taxation of clergy and papal independence. The bull represented the high-water mark of medieval papal claims to universal authority, though it would soon be challenged by the Avignon Papacy and the rise of national monarchies.
events.certainty_note: The bull survives in full text and is precisely dated to November 18, 1302 (some sources list 1301 depending on calendar reckoning). Contemporary documentation is abundant.
cross_references: Part of broader conflict between papacy and secular rulers

events.title: Mansa Musa's pilgrimage preparations in Mali Empire
events.description: Mansa Musa, ruler of the Mali Empire, began preparations for his famous pilgrimage to Mecca, which would occur in 1324. By 1301, Mali was at the height of its power and wealth, controlling trans-Saharan trade routes and accumulating vast gold reserves. The empire's prosperity was built on control of gold mines and salt trade, making it one of the wealthiest polities in the world at this time.

sources.name: Later Islamic chronicles (Ibn Battuta, 14th century; Al-Umari, 14th century)
Oral tradition and griots (West African historical narratives)

events.certainty_note: No contemporary documentation for 1301 specifically. The assignment to this year is inferential based on Mali's documented prosperity in this period and Musa's later prominence. Exact dating of early reign events is uncertain.
cross_references: Leads to Mansa Musa's pilgrimage in 1324

events.title: Yuan Dynasty under Temür Khan: consolidation of Mongol rule in China
events.description: Temür Khan (Kublai Khan's great-grandson) ruled the Yuan Dynasty in China, continuing Mongol administrative integration of Chinese territories. His reign saw relative stability in the Yuan, though underlying tensions between Mongol rulers and Han Chinese subjects persisted. The Yuan maintained the Mongol postal system, military organization, and tributary relationships across Asia.
key_figures: Temür Khan (Kublai Khan's great-grandson, Yuan Emperor)

sources.name: Yuanshi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty, compiled 14th century)

events.certainty_note: Temür Khan's reign dates are well-documented in Yuan records. 1301 falls within his reign (1294–1307).

events.title: Delhi Sultanate under Muhammad of Tughlaq: early reign
events.description: The Delhi Sultanate, under the Tughlaq dynasty, controlled much of northern and central India. Muhammad of Tughlaq's reign (1325–1351) had not yet begun, but his predecessors were consolidating Sultanate power. The Sultanate's military campaigns and administrative structures were expanding Islamic rule across the subcontinent, though Hindu kingdoms remained independent in the south.

sources.name: Tarikh-i Firuz Shahi and other Persian chronicles

events.certainty_note: The Delhi Sultanate's existence and general political structure in 1301 are well-attested, though specific events for this year are not documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Bileća is sometimes dated to 1299 or 1302 rather than 1301; Ottoman sources are late compilations and lack contemporary corroboration. Unam Sanctam is sometimes dated to 1302 depending on calendar systems (Julian vs. Gregorian). The significance of Mansa Musa's early reign is often overstated in popular histories; contemporary West African sources are absent, and his fame derives largely from later Islamic chroniclers' accounts of his 1324 pilgrimage.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1301 is heavily weighted toward Europe and the Islamic world, where written records survive. West African, East Asian, and South Asian sources exist but are often later compilations or administrative records rather than contemporary narratives. The Ottoman sources are particularly problematic: Ottoman chronicles were written centuries after events and often contain legendary material. European sources tend to emphasize papal and dynastic conflicts while marginalizing non-European developments. Archaeological evidence for this year is sparse and rarely datable to a single year.

graph_edges.note: Ottoman expansion in Balkans continues until fall of Constantinople
Unam Sanctam contributed to escalation with Philip IV, leading to Boniface's death
Mali's prosperity under Musa leads to famous pilgrimage
